data.title: The Hong Kong stablecoin test is about to be released. Which one will be able to pass the test on the first try?
data.description:
Recently, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and the Legislative Council have released clear signals that the first batch of stablecoin licenses is entering the final review stage and is expected to be officially issued before the end of March. This news immediately went viral in the cryptocurrency community. Previously, the biggest source of confusion for veterans in the cryptocurrency world was the lack of regulations in the stablecoin sector – either they were secretly setting up operations without a license, or they wanted to enter the market but didn't know where to start due to the lack of clear regulatory guidance, watching opportunities slip away helplessly. Since the Stablecoin Ordinance came into effect in August 2025, Hong Kong has been pushing forward with the licensing process. The core purpose of this upcoming issuance of the first batch of licenses is to address the pain points of compliance chaos and regulatory ambiguity in the stablecoin industry, clarifying the entire process of license application, compliant operation, and risk control. Today, the Sa Jie team will discuss with you the final outcome of Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses. What practical effects will the issuance of these licenses bring? What opportunities and pitfalls should cryptocurrency professionals and investors avoid? We'll delve into this in one article.

I. Why is Hong Kong accelerating the issuance of stablecoin licenses?

First, it's necessary to clarify the positioning and scope of Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation. The Stablecoin Ordinance is a formal local law in Hong Kong, and the issuance of the first batch of licenses is a key measure for its implementation. Its core purpose is to establish a compliance framework and unified regulatory standards for the issuance and operation of stablecoins in Hong Kong, upon which the Hong Kong Monetary Authority will conduct approvals and supervision.

It's important to note that this license currently only applies to institutions registered and operating in Hong Kong; it is not directly applicable to mainland China. However, Hong Kong's regulatory practices have provided an important reference for exploring a compliant path for stablecoins nationwide. At the same time, issuing licenses is also a practical necessity to overcome the industry's compliance dilemma. Previously, the stablecoin sector was plagued by problems such as unlicensed issuance, inaccurate reserves, and money laundering risks, with both regulation and the market lacking clear rules. Hong Kong, through strict approval and screening of compliant entities, has clarified core requirements such as issuance, reserves, and anti-money laundering, resolving the ambiguity of "who can do it and how to do it," and providing clear compliance guidance for the industry. II. Hong Kong Officials: The First Batch of Licenses Will Be Officially Issued by the End of March! (I) Compliance of Underlying Assets According to a report by RTHK on February 11, Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee stated that the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) is actively processing applications for stablecoin issuer licenses, and believes the first batch of licenses will be issued next month. On February 25, Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan also clarified in the Budget 2026 that Hong Kong will issue the first batch of fiat-backed stablecoin issuer licenses in March. The successive pronouncements from the Chief Executive and the Financial Secretary demonstrate the importance Hong Kong places on issuing stablecoin licenses, making the timing of the first batch of licenses no longer market speculation but a predetermined arrangement with clear official backing. Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), previously stated that only a "very small number" of licenses would be issued initially, adhering to the principle of "prudence first, quality over quantity." This statement directly outlines the core tone of this approval process—quality far outweighs quantity. Regulators will not lower entry barriers to increase market participation, but rather prioritize risk control. Our team predicts that the first batch of licenses will be in the single digits, most likely belonging to institutions with mature risk control systems, good market reputation, and the ability to effectively implement anti-money laundering requirements. Many in the cryptocurrency community speculate that Standard Chartered and HSBC will be among those granted stablecoin licenses. While this speculation has not been officially confirmed, it is not unfounded. Both banks are issuers of Hong Kong dollar banknotes and have established mature operating systems in reserve management, liquidity maintenance, and monetary credit, which are highly compatible with the reserve management framework required for stablecoin issuance. From an approval perspective, Hong Kong's stablecoin license review essentially transfers the prudential regulatory standards of traditional finance to the digital finance sector. It requires institutions to meet hard indicators such as capital, reserve assets, and redemption mechanisms, while also emphasizing soft power such as risk control processes, anti-money laundering systems, and corporate governance. The ultimately selected licensees will become compliance benchmarks in Hong Kong's stablecoin market. III. What impact will the issuance of the first batch of licenses have? The confirmation of issuers will completely end the chaotic situation of unlicensed competition in the stablecoin industry. Compliance will become the only bottom line for the industry's survival. Bank-affiliated institutions, with their mature capital, risk control systems, and regulatory trust, will dominate the market landscape. Unlicensed institutions will have to exit or transform into supporting services, significantly increasing the risks of unlicensed operation. Compliant stablecoin issuance effectively addresses the regulatory vacuum and risk chaos in the global stablecoin market, injecting strong confidence into the market. Issuers must strictly comply with the requirements of the Stablecoin Ordinance, meeting stringent standards such as a minimum paid-up capital of HK$25 million, 100% high-quality liquid asset reserves, customer due diligence, and T+0 redemption. Reserve assets are subject to independent custody and real-time auditing, completely eliminating problems such as reserve fraud and fund misappropriation commonly seen in offshore stablecoins. This regulation significantly enhances the sense of security for investors and institutions, attracting global digital financial resources to Hong Kong and laying the foundation for the large-scale application of stablecoins. These stringent regulatory requirements will effectively prevent industry malpractices such as reserve fraud, injecting a strong boost into the market. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority's strict regulations on reserve assets and information disclosure significantly enhance the sense of security for investors and practitioners, attracting global digital financial resources to Hong Kong. In the future, compliant stablecoins will gradually break down the barriers between traditional finance and digital assets, becoming a mainstream tool in cross-border payments, institutional settlements, and other scenarios, opening up new space for industry development. Furthermore, the issuance of licenses will promote the deep integration of stablecoins with traditional finance and the Web3 ecosystem, breaking through the limitations of cross-border payment scenarios. The compliant issuance of stablecoins not only strengthens Hong Kong's global digital finance position but also provides a model for global regulation. Hong Kong has become the world's first international financial center to fully implement a regulatory framework for fiat-backed stablecoins, forming a regulatory tripartite balance with the US and Europe, consolidating its position as Asia's digital asset center. Simultaneously, under the "One Country, Two Systems" framework, its "prudent and inclusive" regulatory model provides a model for domestic digital finance regulation, clarifying the compliance boundaries for practitioners. Hong Kong has also become a core window for domestic practitioners to undergo compliant transformation and connect with the global market, promoting the development of digital finance towards standardization, efficiency, and credibility. IV. How Should Different Entities Respond in Compliance? Having understood the impact of license issuance, let's discuss the most practical question—how should different entities respond? Whether you are an investor, a Web3 company, or a traditional financial institution, you must accurately position yourself and operate within the compliance framework to seize opportunities and avoid pitfalls. (I) Ordinary Investors: Use Compliant Products Rationally and Guard Against Cross-Border Violation Risks For ordinary investors, the core principle is "Stay away from unlicensed products and recognize licensed ones." After the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) publishes the first batch of licensed institutions, it can prioritize stablecoins issued by bank-affiliated institutions. These products are backed by bank credit, have verifiable reserve assets, guaranteed redemption mechanisms, and their asset safety is guaranteed by regulators. At the same time, it is crucial to remember the principle of "risk at your own risk" and avoid blindly speculating on the stablecoin concept. The core value of stablecoins is payment and settlement, not speculation; their investment value should be viewed rationally. Furthermore, investors must pay attention to avoiding cross-border violations. According to the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies" jointly issued by the People's Bank of China and eight other ministries, stablecoins do not have legal tender status and cannot be used as currency. Related transactions and intermediary services within China remain illegal financial activities. If one uses RMB to purchase Hong Kong stablecoins and then exchanges them for foreign currency overseas, it is highly likely to be considered a deliberate attempt to circumvent foreign exchange controls, constituting "disguised foreign exchange trading" as defined in the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate's "Judicial Interpretation on Handling Criminal Cases of Illegal Foreign Exchange Trading," directly crossing the criminal red line of illegal business operations. It must be remembered that "compliance" is territorial; a Hong Kong license is not an "exemption" from mainland foreign exchange controls, and cross-border "match-fixing" will ultimately face severe legal consequences. (II) Web3 Companies: Abandoning Independent Licensing and Shifting to Collaborative Adaptation For Web3 companies, if they cannot obtain the first batch of licenses, they should promptly choose a strategy. Either they can participate in stablecoin sandbox testing to accumulate compliant operational experience and wait for subsequent license issuance windows; or they can cooperate with licensed institutions in Hong Kong to provide technical support, scenario building, and other supporting services, relying on the compliance qualifications of the licensed institutions to legally conduct business. For example, Web3 companies focusing on cross-border payment technology can cooperate with licensed banks to provide technical support for stablecoins to be implemented in cross-border payment scenarios; companies focusing on digital asset custody can cooperate with licensed institutions to provide compliant custody services. This avoids the risks of operating without a license while sharing the dividends of the stablecoin industry's development, making it the optimal choice for small and medium-sized Web3 companies. (III) Traditional Financial Institutions: Seizing First-Mover Opportunities and Deploying Application Scenarios Traditional financial institutions, especially banks, are the biggest winners of this first batch of stablecoin licenses. They can leverage their advantages in capital, risk control, and customer resources to fully promote their applications for Hong Kong dollar stablecoin licenses. After obtaining the license, they should quickly deploy stablecoin application scenarios, such as cross-border payments, institutional settlements, and asset tokenization, to realize the financial infrastructure value of stablecoins. Non-bank financial institutions, such as securities firms and asset management companies, can form consortia with banks to jointly apply for stablecoin licenses, or cooperate with licensed banks to expand stablecoin-related businesses. For example, asset management companies can launch digital asset wealth management products based on the stablecoins of licensed banks, and securities firms can provide compliant channels for stablecoin trading, achieving mutual benefit and win-win results. Meanwhile, all traditional financial institutions must be careful not to provide stablecoin exchange or trading services to mainland residents, to avoid crossing the regulatory red line in mainland China. The issuance of the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong is not the end for the cryptocurrency industry, but the beginning of compliant development. It marks the stablecoin industry's complete departure from the "unlicensed chaos" and its entry into a new stage of "compliance-driven" development, giving cryptocurrency practitioners hope for compliant development. It is worth noting that many users are currently accustomed to using offshore stablecoins such as USDT, and the cost of migrating to compliant stablecoins is high. This has become a significant challenge for the initial promotion of compliant stablecoins in Hong Kong. In the future, with the continuous improvement of the compliant stablecoin ecosystem, the ongoing expansion of application scenarios, and the gradual deepening of user education, this migration challenge is expected to be gradually resolved. The market acceptance of compliant stablecoins will also steadily increase, truly achieving a win-win situation for both digital financial innovation and financial security.

data.title: Messari: A Comprehensive Analysis of Pharos and Its Underlying Mechanisms
data.description:
Authors: Youssef Haidar, Matt Kreiser; Source: Messari; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Core Insights

  • Pharos is a modular Layer-1 blockchain designed to become a global infrastructure serving Real-World Assets (RWA). It was founded by a former executive who led the blockchain infrastructure team at Ant Group.

  • Unlike other public chains that only parallelize the execution process, Pharos treats the entire block lifecycle, consensus mechanism, transaction execution, data storage, and data availability as a unified concurrent process, aiming to achieve a processing capacity of 30,000 transactions per second (TPS) on the mainnet.

  • Pharos Store embeds Merkle trees directly into the storage kernel, reducing the I/O path from 8 to 10 disk reads to 1 to 3. This design directly addresses a little-known performance bottleneck—one that limits overall throughput even on the fastest parallel public chains. Pharos is compatible with both the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) and WebAssembly (WASM) within a Single Deterministic Runtime Environment (DTVM), allowing Solidity smart contracts to directly call Rust contracts without cross-chain bridges or incurring additional overhead across virtual machines.

  • Through the Dedicated Processing Network (SPN), developers can quickly build application-specific execution layers for high-load scenarios (such as derivatives trading and zero-knowledge proof verification); this architecture inherits mainnet security through a native restaking mechanism, eliminating the need to build a completely new set of verification nodes from scratch.

Introduction

Pharos is a high-performance, modular Layer-1 blockchain designed to create a global infrastructure for serving real-world assets (RWA). The network achieves sub-second block generation speeds and can support billions of concurrent users. Its mission is to build an inclusive financial system, combining the processing speed of Web2 systems with the decentralized security of public blockchains. Pharos adheres to a quality-first, quantity-first philosophy in the asset ecosystem, aiming to release liquidity for underserved groups and established institutions.

Pharos is a high-performance, modular Layer-1 blockchain designed to build a global infrastructure for serving real-world assets (RWA).

The core difference between Pharos and other Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) networks lies in its highly parallel (DP) design. Unlike other public chains that only parallelize transaction execution, Pharos can run concurrently throughout the entire block lifecycle (data availability, execution, settlement, and consensus), and further enhances performance through dedicated hardware. By eliminating hidden bottlenecks in each stage of the block lifecycle, Pharos can stably achieve a processing capacity of 30,000 transactions per second (TPS) and 2 gigabits per second, ensuring the network can support the global concurrent demands of billions of users. Following the success of the AtlanticOcean testnet (launched in October 2025), Pharos is currently preparing for the mainnet launch and token generation event (TGE) in the second quarter of 2026. Project Background Pharos was founded in November 2024 by Alex Zhang and Wish Wu, both former senior executives of Ant Group's blockchain infrastructure team. Alex Zhang previously served as CEO of ZAN, a Web3 subsidiary of Ant Group Digital Technology, and also as CTO of AntChain. Wish Wu previously served as Chief Security Officer of ZAN, possessing extensive experience in institutional security and compliance. Pharos was established as an independently incubated project, aiming to upgrade the aforementioned technological infrastructure into a decentralized public Layer 1 blockchain. The founding team boasts strong technical capabilities, with members holding backgrounds from institutions such as Microsoft, PayPal, Stanford University, and Ripple. In November 2024, Pharos completed an $8 million seed funding round, co-led by Hack VC and Lightspeed Faction. Leveraging this funding, Pharos has entered into a strategic partnership with ZAN, focusing on node infrastructure, security, and hardware acceleration to ensure the network possesses institutional-grade reliability. Pharos optimizes the entire block lifecycle as a parallel process. Its architecture is based on the core principle that if only the execution phase is parallelized, the network will eventually encounter bottlenecks in storage I/O, consensus, or data availability. To eliminate such bottlenecks, Pharos employs a modular technology stack, achieving decoupling of execution, consensus, and settlement, and is supported by a customized storage engine and a dual virtual machine environment.

Consensus Layer

Traditional Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus mechanisms rely on a single node producing blocks, which not only creates a performance ceiling but also introduces the risk of single point of failure. Pharos eliminates this limitation through a fully asynchronous BFT protocol that does not rely on fixed timing assumptions, allowing validator nodes to dynamically advance the process based on actual network conditions, rather than relying on a preset timeout mechanism. Most round-based BFT protocols require the network to wait for confirmation of previous rounds before continuing, resulting in network throughput being limited by worst-case network latency. Pharos decouples the block proposal phase from the block commit phase, allowing validators to process transactions based on real-time network capacity. This design avoids network stagnation in volatile environments, maximizing throughput without sacrificing security. Even in fully asynchronous network environments where message transmission times are completely unpredictable, the protocol still guarantees network liveness. To prevent duplicate transactions from crowding the network, the system uses a deterministic mapping algorithm to assign each transaction to a designated validator node. The diagram above clearly illustrates this mechanism: transactions in the transaction pool are processed in partitions. Validator node 1 processes transactions 1 and 2; validator node 2 processes transactions 3 and 4; validator node 3 processes transaction 5; while validator node 4, which was not assigned a transaction in this round, remains idle and does not broadcast redundant data. Subsequently, each active validator node packages its assigned transactions into an independent proposal. The end result is that network resources expand linearly with the addition of validator nodes (the total bandwidth of proposals roughly doubles as the number of validator nodes doubles), rather than creating more idle nodes. After validator nodes submit proposals in parallel, all proposals enter a high-density full-node voting process. If at least two-thirds of the validator nodes reach a consensus on the proposal, the network will merge reliable broadcasting and consensus voting into a streamlined process, completing block confirmation in just three rounds of communication. The final output is a deduplicated and sorted final confirmed block. The core of the Pharos execution layer is the Deterministic Virtual Machine (DTVM) technology stack, which replaces the traditional serial processing mode with a parallel dual-virtual machine architecture. DTVM natively supports both EVM and WASM execution modes in a unified runtime environment, eliminating the need to deploy independent virtual machines and achieving seamless interoperability between Solidity contracts and contracts written in languages ​​such as Rust, Go, and C++. To ensure strict determinism across different hardware environments, DTVM compiles all bytecode into a deterministic intermediate representation (dMIR)—this unified, cross-platform conversion layer eliminates non-deterministic behaviors such as floating-point ambiguity and undefined exception handling. dMIR is independent of the host system, enforcing standardized termination conditions, strict numerical calculation rules, and a fixed-size virtual call stack (8MB space, maximum call depth of 1024 layers), ensuring completely consistent state replication regardless of whether the node runs on x86 or ARM architecture. Because dMIR serves as a unified backend for multiple bytecode frontends, a single optimized Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler can simultaneously support EVM and WASM, and may even support RISC V contracts in the future, avoiding the fragmentation problems and redundant virtual machine overhead that plague multi-virtual machine architectures. Only modules successfully compiled into dMIRs are allowed to execute, making this intermediate representation itself a deterministic gatekeeper. To reduce the latency caused by traditional JIT compilation, DTVM integrates the Zeta engine. Most blockchain virtual machines face a dilemma: either complete all compilation early but postpone deployment, or compile on the first call but delay execution. Zeta takes a different approach, using function-level compilation instead of contract-level compilation. During contract deployment, the engine verifies the contract, generates dMIRs, and asynchronously begins compiling individual functions in the background. If a function is called before compilation is complete, a lightweight stub triggers just-in-time compilation, automatically replacing the code so that all subsequent calls directly execute the optimized native code. This results in an initial call latency of only 0.95 milliseconds, with native code running from the second call onwards. The Pharos Pipelining architecture integrates the above modules, breaking down the sequential block lifecycle into multiple concurrent stages. In conventional blockchains, blocks must sequentially go through proposal, execution, and final confirmation, with each stage waiting for the previous one to complete. Pharos, however, dynamically allocates CPU and disk I/O resources through a 64-core framework, allowing transaction execution, Merkle tree computation, and final state confirmation to run synchronously in overlapping pipelines, ensuring that hardware resources are always used efficiently and are never idle. The architecture also supports a flexible, multi-tiered final confirmation mechanism. Pharos separates order final confirmation (permanent transaction ordering), transaction final confirmation (deterministic execution results), and block final confirmation (accessible fully confirmed blocks). Applications can obtain confirmation information about transaction ordering and execution results before the block is fully confirmed on-chain, which is a valuable user experience optimization for latency-sensitive scenarios such as transactions and games; while underlying infrastructure components such as oracles and indexers wait for the block to be fully confirmed. This architecture enables Pharos to achieve a throughput of 500,000 TPS in optimized environments, with latency reduced by 30%–50% compared to traditional serial pipeline models. The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) has historically been inefficient for computationally intensive tasks; its 256-bit word length, stack-based architecture, and lack of native support for modern hardware characteristics constitute a performance ceiling that is difficult to overcome. Ph-WASM is a runtime environment built by Pharos specifically for WebAssembly. It can run in parallel with the EVM to handle high-throughput tasks such as AI model scheduling, on-chain perpetual contract transactions, and zero-knowledge proof verification. Through advanced compiler optimizations such as Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD) vectorization and opcode fusion, it maintains high efficiency and low resource consumption for both CPU-intensive and I/O-intensive operations. The practical significance of this design is that developers can write performance-critical logic in languages ​​such as Rust or C++ and deploy it to Ph-WASM, while keeping existing Solidity contracts running on the EVM. Since both virtual machines ultimately compile to the same dMIR layer, Solidity contracts can natively call Rust contracts without cross-chain bridges, nested virtual machine execution, or inter-process communication overhead. This means that liquidity and composability remain consistent across both runtime environments. For example, a decentralized finance (DeFi) protocol can run user-facing liquidity pool logic in Solidity to ensure ecosystem compatibility; at the same time, it can migrate the pricing engine to a Rust contract on Ph-WASM to achieve the extreme throughput performance required for dynamic real-time applications.

Storage Layer

State bloat and slow disk I/O are the hidden killers of on-chain scalability.

State bloat and slow disk I/O are the hidden killers of on-chain scalability.

Even with the fastest execution engine, systems can still experience congestion if they need to wait for traditional Merkle-Patricia Trees (MPTs) to read data from disk. For example, Ethereum's MPT requires 8 to 10 independent disk reads to access the state of a single account, and its hash-based addressing method triggers frequent database compression, consuming significant disk bandwidth. As the network expands to hundreds of millions of accounts, these costs accumulate, ultimately becoming the core bottleneck limiting throughput, rather than the execution or consensus stages. Pharos Store is a blockchain-native storage engine built on the concept of efficient, trustworthy, and universally applicable log-structured storage (LETUS), aiming to eliminate these bottlenecks at the architectural level. Its core innovation is Authentication Data Structure Pushdown (ADS Pushdown). Unlike the standard two-layer design that overlays Merkle trees onto independent key-value databases, Pharos Store embeds the Merkle tree directly within the storage engine. This reduces the I/O path from 8–10 disk reads to 1–3, and this structural optimization amplifies with each transaction processed by the network. The engine organizes data around three dedicated structures: Incrementally Encoded Multi-Version Merkle Tree (DMM-Tree): A high-fanout Merkle tree with a built-in incremental encoding mechanism, meaning it only persists modified state changes, rather than rewriting the entire node. Log Structure Versioned Page Store (LSVPS): Provides a page-level index abstraction between memory and disk for the DMM-Tree, using monotonically increasing version numbers instead of hash addressing. This version-based index eliminates the heavy compression cycles that plague traditional LSM tree backends, reducing disk bandwidth consumption by 96.5%. Versioned Data Log Streaming (VDLS): Stores user metadata in an append-only log format, ensuring data integrity and supporting rapid recovery after node crashes. According to team data, Pharos Store can reduce storage overhead by 80%, and its I/O throughput is 15.8 times that of the Ethereum MPT+LevelDB architecture. Especially for parallel execution, the engine supports concurrent reads, multi-threaded Merkle hash calculations, and non-blocking writes, ensuring that the storage layer can keep up with the execution layer speed, rather than becoming a performance bottleneck. The system also supports tiered storage, automatically migrating old block data from high-speed SSDs to low-cost archive storage; boundary scan-based pruning technology has reduced storage usage by over 42% in production testing.

Network Layer

All communication in the Pharos system is supported by the network layer, using an optimized P2P gossip protocol to achieve low-latency message propagation. The system achieves adaptive bandwidth allocation based on real-time network load, ensuring efficient distribution of transactions and data even under high-pressure scenarios.

Dedicated Processing Network (SPN)

Pharos introduces the Dedicated Processing Network (SPN) to achieve modular, application-oriented expansion. SPN is essentially a customized execution layer that inherits Pharos' security guarantees while operating semi-autonomously with independent consensus parameters and business logic. Developers can configure SPN for computationally intensive tasks that are difficult or too costly to implement on general public blockchains, such as fully homomorphic encryption (FHE), multi-party secure computation (MPC), AI model inference, and high-frequency trading.

SPN achieves secure launch through a native restaking mechanism. Pharos mainnet validators can stake native tokens and obtain liquidity staking credentials, which can then be restaking to one or more SPNs. This model builds a shared security system, making the launch of dedicated subnets both secure and financially efficient, without forcing each new network to recruit an independent set of validators from scratch.

Users can transfer assets and data between SPN and the main chain using the SPN interoperability protocol, which includes three core components: mailbox, registry, and cross-chain bridge.

Users can use the SPN interoperability protocol to transfer assets and data between SPN and the main chain. This protocol includes three core components: mailbox, registry, and cross-chain bridge.

Unlike general Layer 2 networks, this protocol is deeply integrated with the Pharos mainnet, enabling low-latency message relay and atomic asset transfers, avoiding the liquidity fragmentation problem common in multi-chain architectures. The cross-SPN communication process is as follows: A user initiates a cross-SPN transaction on SPN1, aiming to execute it in the message queue of SPN2. The relay node sends the transaction, its cryptographic proof, and block header to the mainnet. The mainnet verifies the transaction's authenticity and records it in its mailbox, serving as the authoritative and trusted data source for cross-SPN messages. SPN2 retrieves the message from the mailbox and records it in its local mailbox, completing the execution handover. The process is managed by two core smart contracts: the SPN adapter handles message verification and cross-SPN routing at the protocol layer; the SPN manager manages the lifecycle, registry status, and governance, ensuring that each SPN configuration is consistent with the overall Pharos network. Together, they achieve atomic execution and verifiable data sharing across SPNs without relying on trusted intermediaries. This design also includes a built-in emergency exit mechanism, allowing users to withdraw their assets back to the mainnet at any time, regardless of the SPN operator's actions. This ensures censorship resistance, a crucial security guarantee for high-value scenarios such as DeFi derivatives and institutional asset management.

Ecosystem

To welcome 2026 The Pharos Foundation, the non-profit organization responsible for overseeing the development and ecosystem building of the Pharos network, has established a comprehensive ecosystem encompassing Real-World Assets (RWA), Bitcoin Finance (BTCFi), decentralized exchanges (DEX), perpetual contract decentralized exchanges (Perp DEX), prediction markets, liquidity staking (LST), yield farming and automation, artificial intelligence and smart agent banking, lending protocols, as well as infrastructure such as indexing services, remote procedure calls (RPC), oracles, multi-signature wallets, block explorers, security services, cross-chain interoperability, and wallets. This ecosystem centers on "RealFi," which refers to an institutional-grade financial system built on-chain based on real-world assets, rather than relying on native crypto assets to obtain decentralized finance (DeFi) yields. RealFi adheres to the principles of openness and inclusiveness, making various real-world assets available to all users through issuance. For example, Centrifuge will issue its tokenized US Treasury bond product JTRSY and AAA-rated structured credit product JAAA on the Pharos network. To achieve open access to RealFi in the context of current ecosystem fragmentation remaining a major obstacle to institutional adoption, the Pharos Foundation announced the establishment of the RealFi Alliance. Within the Pharos network and this consortium framework: Chainlink, as the authoritative cross-chain infrastructure, ensures secure message transmission and data integrity. Pharos also uses Chainlink Data Streams to provide price data for its RWA market. LayerZero provides interoperability support, and TopNod provides a secure self-custodied wallet. Centrifuge issues freely transferable and fully composable RWA products through its deRWA token standard, encapsulating existing tokenized securities into freely transferable tokens, achieving full compatibility with DeFi protocols. Anchorage Digital, as the first federally regulated crypto bank in the United States, provides institutional-grade asset custody, token minting, and asset distribution services, including token-related services for venture capital firms participating in Pharos' upcoming TGE. R25 will launch a protocol powered by RWA, focusing on structured lending and transparent yield design. Faroo will provide a liquidity staking protocol for RWA on the Pharos network. The Real Finance Consortium will expand in batches, with subsequent members selected based on asset quality, technological maturity, and ecosystem fit. In addition, Pharos announced a $10 million RealFi Builder Incubation Program to support early-stage teams building DeFi applications and infrastructure on the Pharos network. Partners for this incubation program include Hack VC, Draper Dragon, Lightspeed Faction, and Centrifuge. In summary, Pharos' core philosophy is that simply parallelizing transaction execution is far from sufficient. By designing the entire block lifecycle, consensus, execution, storage, and data availability as a concurrent process, this network attempts to address the structural bottleneck that has long limited the throughput of L1 public chains. Its DTVM technology stack is compatible with EVM and WASM in a unified deterministic runtime, while Pharos Store aims to reduce storage I/O from 8–10 disk reads to 1–3, directly addressing a long-neglected constraint on on-chain scalability. The Dedicated Processing Network (SPN) promises to provide a modular scaling path while avoiding liquidity fragmentation in independent execution environments. With TGE and the mainnet expected to launch in Q2 2026, the project's ultimate future will depend on its ability to translate its architectural design into actual network performance and the successful implementation of the RealFi ecosystem on the Pharos network.

data.title: Europe Meets US Tech CEOs Including Google, Meta, OpenAI and Amazon to Review AI Power and Fair Competition
data.description:
Silicon Valley Braces For Europe's Antitrust Chief

The high-rise offices of San Francisco are hosting a visitor this week who carries the weight of European law.

Teresa Ribera, the EU’s antitrust chief, has landed in California to look directly into the engines of the world’s most powerful tech companies.

Her schedule is a marathon of high-stakes meetings, starting Tuesday with Alphabet’s Sundar Pichai, Meta’s Mark Zuckerberg, and OpenAI’s Sam Altman.

By Wednesday, she moves on to Amazon’s Andy Jassy.

This is not a social call.

It is a deep dive into the "full AI stack," from the data used to train chatbots to the massive cloud networks that keep them running.

🚨 TECH: EU ANTITRUST CHIEF MEETS PICHAI, ZUCKERBERG, ALTMAN AND JASSY IN SAN FRANCISCO THIS WEEK

Brussels just flew to Silicon Valley. And she's not here to sightsee.

Per Reuters, EU competition commissioner @Teresaribera is holding first-time meetings with @Google CEO Sundar… pic.twitter.com/9zaucO29Pb

— BSCN (@BSCNews) March 24, 2026

Can Big Tech Control Every Layer Of AI

Europe is worried that a few companies are becoming too dominant by controlling every stage of the AI process.

Ribera has already launched several investigations into Google and Meta, fearing that these giants might use their platforms to push their own AI tools while locking out smaller rivals.

Because companies like OpenAI, Nvidia, Meta, and Google have spent billions on infrastructure, computing power has become a primary weapon in business.

Ribera’s week-long trip aims to determine if this concentration of power has already gone too far, potentially stifling competition before it can even begin.

Why Brussels Is Tired Of Washington's Complaints

While Ribera talks shop in San Francisco, a verbal battle is heating up between Brussels and Washington.

U.S. officials have been vocal in their distaste for the EU’s digital rulebooks, specifically the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the Digital Markets Act (DMA).

Andrew Puzder, the U.S. Ambassador to the EU, suggested that a vote this week on a 2025 transatlantic trade deal might be a chance to ease these digital rules.

However, European lawmakers are showing zero interest in negotiating.

"We signed the agreement in August and every day since then the US has been in compliance."@USAmbEU Andrew Puzder tells #EuropeToday it is the EU that has yet to be in compliance with the EU-US trade deal, urging EU lawmakers to pass it this Thursday.

Full interview: pic.twitter.com/jJG5PSXXUK

— euronews (@euronews) March 24, 2026

German lawmaker Andreas Schwab noted,

“There is a certain level of tiredness in Brussels when it comes to responding to these talking points from Washington.”

Is Digital Law A Trade Negotiation Chip

The EU insists its laws are about protecting people, not attacking American business.

Andreas Schwab clarified that the Digital Markets Act “is not an opening bid in a trade negotiation; it is a settled legal reality.”

He argued that regardless of who is delivering the message from the U.S., the script is always the same: they call European law an "attack" while ignoring that the rules were created through years of democratic debate.

Supporting this stance, Italian lawmaker Brando Benifei stated,

“I don’t see any political appetite in the European Parliament but not even in Council for scaling back our digital legislation dealing with malicious content, manipulation or unfair treatment of startups and consumers alike.”

Will The Trade Deal Survive The Digital Friction

Tensions are reaching a boiling point as the European Parliament prepares for a Thursday vote on the 2025 trade deal.

EU “not one day” in compliance with EU–US trade deal, @USAmbEU tells #EuropeToday.

In an exclusive interview, Andrew Puzder added it would be “economic malpractice” not to pass the deal as EU lawmakers vote on it this Thursday.

Watch: https://t.co/58kZa2ISZhpic.twitter.com/OyC2Xlsrlc

— euronews (@euronews) March 24, 2026

The conflict has taken on a political edge, with U.S. Democrat Jamie Raskin telling the Internal Market Committee that some attacks on EU rules are fueled by a "MAGA-aligned agenda."

Raskin claimed the Trump administration “works hard to promote the MAGA movement in Europe under the guise of defending free speech,” while simultaneously restricting it at home.

This follows a February move by the House Judiciary Committee, led by Jim Jordan, which labelled the DSA a “foreign censorship tool” and targeted nearly 30 EU officials responsible for its enforcement.

data.title: People miss DeepSeek
data.description:
Editor's Note: Tokens are reshaping the value coordinates of the AI ​​era. Is it the engine of an efficiency revolution, or a hidden reef of uncontrolled costs? This issue will examine the economic logic of the "new oil" of the AI ​​era from the perspective of token cost reduction.

"Iron Shrimp" Fed by Tokens.

Author: Su Yang, Tencent Technology

Recent discussions about tokens have been quite surreal.

Discussions about the Chinese translation of "Token" are ubiquitous on social media—from "Ceographies" and "Intelligence Elements" to even humorous versions like "Wisdom Roots." Tokens aren't a new concept; they've coexisted with neural networks since the first day of large-scale model deployment. However, it wasn't until OpenClaw (commonly known as "Lobster") achieved widespread adoption among users that various agent applications began to bring tokens into the public eye. I believe there are two key issues: its consumption is too high, and its price is too expensive. I remember when OpenAI released GPT-5.4, some users reported consuming $80 worth of tokens just by saying "Hello." At the time, many people said this usage was outrageous, but with the widespread adoption of OpenClaw, burning through tens of millions of tokens in a single task became commonplace. In contrast, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang, at GTC 2026 and in many subsequent forums, has emphasized the importance of engineers using tokens extensively, even incorporating them into compensation incentives. In one interview, Huang stated, "If an engineer earning $500,000 a year hasn't even used $250,000 worth of tokens, I would be extremely alarmed." The question is, will frantically burning tokens necessarily solve the problem? How many tokens are effective, and what is a reasonable return on investment? Combined with recent foreign media reports, an OpenAI programmer burned through 210 billion tokens in a week, equivalent to 33 Wikipedia entries, but what has this level of consumption ultimately brought? I posted on WeChat Moments, asking if heavy usage like this could get me to P10. A friend immediately commented, "It can help those selling tokens get to P10." Clearly, the effectiveness of this frenzied token burning is questionable, but who the beneficiaries are is certain. Jensen Huang describes Nvidia as the "King of Tokens," possessing the world's most advanced "Token Manufacturing Machine." However, if he relentlessly promotes this, even implying that not using tokens will lead to falling behind, then we can say that: On one hand, Huang wants to fundamentally change the logic of "efficiency assessment" for companies in the AI ​​era; on the other hand, he has indirectly created token anxiety. I. Tokens Are Too Expensive Not long ago, I asked Zhou Hongyi about the issue of "Tokens being too expensive." He said, "There might be some misunderstanding that people think Tokens are expensive, because the backend of large models can be flexibly configured." In his understanding, users can choose models to control costs. "The cost of everyday chat conversations is actually very low. What really consumes Tokens are complex tasks, such as generating videos, creating short dramas, or writing novels." I remember Cheetah Mobile CEO Fu Sheng saying in a video that he optimized the initial daily Token cost of several hundred dollars to the current daily cost of just over ten dollars through some usage techniques. That's 2100 yuan for 30 days, and 25200 yuan for an annual fee. The question is: how many users can afford a daily cost of ten dollars? Compared to commercial consumer-facing (to C) software currently available on the Chinese internet, such as CapCut, high-end memberships only cost around 600 yuan per year, while entertainment-related memberships are roughly 300 yuan. It's practically impossible to find a consumer-grade software with an annual fee exceeding 25,000 yuan. "Most people still wouldn't accept $10 a day; this will filter out a large number of non-paying users," I told Fu Sheng, who didn't deny my assessment. These days, I've also been trying various types of crayfish products, and the costs involved go far beyond tokens. For example, if users need raw images, a dedicated raw image model API is required; if they want to monitor activity, a paid search API is needed. These potential costs will gradually deter most users. While there might be some open-source workarounds to reduce costs, open-source projects indirectly harbor security risks. On March 13th, during the first episode of Tencent Technology's "Shrimp Talk" livestream series, Lambda, a guest from Xuanwu Lab, shared data showing that his average monthly cost for "raising shrimp" (a metaphor for managing shrimp) exceeded 1,000 yuan. Whether comparing this to the annual fees of consumer-grade tools or feedback from industry "shrimp farmers," the claim that "Tokens are too expensive" based on Agent-based Token consumption is valid. II. Storage Bottlenecks and Efficiency Black Holes Tokens, simply put, are the basic units for information processing in large language models—user input prompts, model outputs answers; every word and every punctuation mark contributes to Token consumption, essentially representing a computational cost. In the past, there were many metrics used to calculate the total cost of ownership (TCO) of computing power, including Flops/W (total FLOPS/W) for energy efficiency and cost/Flops for average calculations. In this year's "Token Economics," Token/W has gradually become a consensus. "The cost of each of our tokens is the lowest in the world," said Jensen Huang at GTC. However, no matter how cheap it is, and regardless of the unit of computing, it is still a quantifiable input cost, involving R&D costs, hardware costs, deployment costs, energy consumption costs, and operating costs. In other words, cost reduction revolves around these aspects. One piece of bad news for token cost reduction is the skyrocketing price of memory. Taking HBM memory as an example, it is a key component supporting large model training and inference. At the same time, the surge in inference data has also triggered a simultaneous increase in storage demand. In the first quarter of 2026, DRAM prices rose by more than 50% quarter-on-quarter, while NAND prices saw a peak increase of 150%. Jensen Huang and Lisa Su have both declared they will take as much HBM as possible, and memory manufacturers like Samsung and Micron have disclosed that they have signed strategic long-term contracts with their top clients for up to five years. The article "100 Days of Memory Price Surge Forces the Death of Budget Phones" mentioned that in the consumer market, inventory of budget phones may have to be discontinued. However, cloud providers are also currently suffering from rising prices due to this issue. The most optimistic industry forecast is that memory prices will fall by 2028, while a more pessimistic forecast is for 2030. As long as memory prices don't fall, token price reductions lack a crucial external lever. Improved model capabilities can also be seen as another lever for price reductions. "Some small 8B models are now approaching the capabilities of full-scale large models," said an academic researcher. In this regard, Mianbi AI, in collaboration with a Tsinghua University team, proposed the concept of Densing Law in *Nature*, emphasizing that the capability density of large models increases exponentially over time, roughly doubling every 3.5 months, while the number of parameters required for equivalent performance halves every 3.5 months. A domestic AI chip industry practitioner also emphasized that good model capabilities and small scale can drive down costs. "Look at the price of domestic open-source large model tokens; they are basically positively correlated with the model size." Several domestic computing power practitioners stated that **improving MFU (Mean Functionality Fusion) also brings room for cost reduction**, in addition to inference optimizations in architecture, memory, and other aspects. "MFU (Mean Functionality Fusion) isn't so much related to the model itself, but mainly to the operators and scheduling strategies," said another domestic in-memory computing chip industry professional. "Currently, the average inference MFU of mainstream large models is around 30%, and after optimization, it can exceed 50%, which is estimated to save 50% of the cost." In other words, the industry hasn't fully utilized GPU performance—spending 100% of the GPU cost, now only using less than a third of the computing power. However, while MFU improvement can lead to a decrease in the cost per token, whether this will be passed on to end consumers depends on the commercial considerations of large model providers. If used to wage a price war, it is undoubtedly an effective lever. III. Another Price War Price wars in China's large models are not without precedent. In 2024, a fierce price war erupted among domestic vendors. This coincided with the launch of DeepSeek-V2, where every million tokens input cost 1 yuan and output 2 yuan, a price equivalent to one percent of GPT-4-Turbo. The key to DeepSeek's price reduction at that time lay in inference optimization—the MoE sparse architecture significantly reduced computational load, and MLA multi-head potential attention compressed the KV cache by over 90%. Following DeepSeek's price cuts, Alibaba, ByteDance, and others subsequently entered the fray, leading to a period where "tokens were free." At a conference years ago, Wang Xiaochuan discussed price wars, arguing that they were fundamentally different from previous group-buying and ride-hailing wars. He stated, "This price war is a direct supply of productivity; it's a price war in the B2B market." At the time, Wang Xiaochuan also emphasized that even if large companies incurred losses in the short term, they could potentially achieve profitability within a year. "With improved reasoning efficiency, subsidies led to a very significant increase in users," said an insider from a large model company that participated in the previous price war. "It probably cost several hundred million yuan." However, this round of token consumption, with simultaneous surges in demand from both B2B and B2C, actually possesses the conditions to change production relations, similar to the group-buying and ride-hailing wars, yet the market has shown surprising silence. The aforementioned insider from a major model involved in the price war believes that once the model's specific capabilities mature and a stable user base is established, there may be little incentive for companies to engage in price wars again. "Token consumption isn't on the same scale as in 2024. In this situation, to wage a price war, the ARR revenue of existing users will be forced to bleed," said the aforementioned domestic AI chip industry professional. "It's unnecessary. The incremental growth brought by a price war is uncertain. Cutting the existing user base first is a bad business strategy." According to Artificial Analysis's tracking data, the API unit price of domestic models is already cheap enough, but this cheapness is far from sufficient for the massive consumption by agents. As mentioned earlier, impacted by the hardware costs of memory and storage, domestic cloud providers are currently facing the challenge of price increases, with little possibility of price reductions in the short term. "This is a continuation of the price wars of the past two years, with domestic manufacturers having a clear price advantage over North American companies. However, everyone understands that acquiring users is a protracted battle, not something that can be resolved in one or two price wars," added the aforementioned domestic in-memory computing chip industry professional. IV. "Welding" the Model to the Chip To address the cost issues caused by the rampant consumption of tokens, some users have begun to try deploying models locally. To date, many users have configured local models for "Xiaolongxia" on Mac Mini. However, this solution will continuously drive up the cost of token usage in the short term, and local deployment itself has a high barrier to entry, while the capabilities of open-source models may not meet user needs. For entry-level users, some manufacturers are attempting to launch EdgeClaw hardware, adding a security narrative to the hardware business. This is a worthwhile direction to explore, but its timing seems ill-timed given the rising memory prices. Previously, a Mini PC entrepreneur stated that price increases impact the entire industry. "Previously, users thought 'it's too expensive,' but now they simply don't even look at it; they don't care how much memory or hard drive space you have," the entrepreneur said. Meanwhile, some brands are also launching barebone systems (without memory or storage) on e-commerce platforms, with prices starting below 2000 yuan. While these lack a "security narrative," they represent the first hurdle that startups like EdgeClaw must overcome. For edge AI hardware like the "crayfish" (referring to a specific product or service), the biggest challenge is still the Mac Mini. Apple's supply chain power and profit margins can support the Mac Mini's extremely high cost-performance ratio, making it difficult for startups to tell a compelling story. Remember the "all-in-one" PCs that DeepSeek was all the rage in early 2025? Do you see their stories still existing in the industry today? Besides integrated hardware solutions like all-in-one PCs, **some startups are also trying to innovate from the underlying chip level.** In February, the Taalas team launched a brand-new chip, the HC1. Based on the TSMC N6 process, this chip has a die size of 815mm², a transistor density of only 53B, and can run the Llama 3.1 8B model on a single chip. Most importantly, it boasts a single-user TPS (Token/s) output of 16960/s, an exceptionally high figure, thanks to the HC1's design. The Taalas team used Mask ROM to hard-encode and solidify the Llama 3.1 8B model weights onto the silicon die. The interconnects in the chip's metal layers act like neural network connections, essentially "soldering" the model onto the chip. This achieves physical integration of computation and storage, completely eliminating HBM/DRAM data transfer and breaking the memory wall limitation. While its TPS performance is outstanding, its weakness also stems from the fact that the model is "soldered" onto the chip. This means that it can only run a fixed version of a fixed model; the weights cannot be changed, the structure cannot be altered, and changing the model requires a complete re-fabrication. You can also understand this as dedicated chip for a specific purpose. V. In Conclusion All discussions are based on the cost of token usage—the high cost is not the unit price, but the exponential increase in token usage due to heavy tasks. I once tried using crayfish to generate GIFs with a specific timestamp. During a conversation with a colleague, he said, "My colleague makes these GIFs; they take half a minute each, by hand." While this example isn't very typical, spending several yuan to make just a few GIFs is clearly not economical.

The cost of creating GIFs using the DeepSeek API

To change this, either cheaper token pricing or minimizing token consumption is needed. This depends on model-level optimization and innovation in inference hardware.

To change this, either cheaper token pricing or minimizing token consumption is required. This depends on model-level optimization and innovation in inference hardware.

However, regardless of the circumstances, with the total cost of token usage failing to decrease and the effective return on investment unclear, the frantic promotion of token consumption, even emphasizing its link to performance, is arguably creating not only token anxiety but also AI anxiety. Looking further back, Huang (Zhou Hongyi) even urged tech industry leaders to speak cautiously to avoid triggering irrational public panic about artificial intelligence technology. This is akin to telling the entire industry: stop suppressing AI and creating panic; you should all burn through tokens. But the question remains: who will solve the price problem? Will it be the long-awaited DeepSeek V4? I remember in 2017, there was a viral article titled "The People Miss Zhou Hongyi." Now, the people probably miss the token price war and DeepSeek. At least for the "shrimp farmers," this is most likely the case.

data.title: Breaking news: OpenAI shuts down Sora, Disney's $1 billion deal falls through.
data.description:
Sora passed away at the age of 1 year and 4 months.

Author:Jiang Yu

Sora's closure marks the end of the globally stunning "Sora moment".

Sora's closure marks the end of the "Sora moment" that amazed the world.

Sora's closure marks the end of the globally stunning "Sora moment".

Sora's closure marks the end of the globally stunning "Sora moment".

... On March 25th, Zhidongxi reported that OpenAI's video generation product, Sora, announced its shutdown via its official account early this morning, closing its video generation application and Sora 2 model API service. At the same time, its $1 billion collaboration with Disney also fell through. This product, almost synonymous with the field of AI video generation, has now been put on hold. The news was met with swift farewell from the community. Many netizens shared a picture of a "Sora tombstone," showing a group of people gathered around a grave, with "Sora" engraved on the tombstone, accompanied by the logo of its competitor, "Seedance 2.0," clearly a sarcastic remark. Just yesterday, Seedance 2.0 was officially launched globally. Some netizens also expressed regret, stating that "Sora originally had great potential." The well-known AI blogger NIK bluntly stated, "As predicted," and dug up an old post predicting that "Sora would disappear," claiming that its fate was foreshadowed. In the download curve he previously shared, Sora's popularity steadily declined. According to official information, this shutdown covers two core capabilities: the standalone Sora application for users and the Sora 2 model API for developers. Both ordinary creators and developers building video workflows based on Sora will lose their direct access point. The Wall Street Journal further revealed that, in addition to standalone applications and APIs, OpenAI will also cease supporting video-related functions in ChatGPT. The Sora team stated that they will announce the discontinuation timeline for the applications and APIs later, and will provide solutions for saving and migrating user work. It is worth noting that this decision came quite suddenly. Just this week, OpenAI was still continuously updating the Sora product. Looking back at its trajectory, Sora ignited the industry upon its debut in early 2024, considered a key milestone in AI video generation. Following this, Sora 2 was launched in 2025, with iOS and Android apps available. The iOS version even topped the App Store download charts, and the product evolved from a tool into an "AI version of TikTok." However, the competitive window didn't last long. With the rapid iteration of models like Runway, Luma, Google's Veo series, and domestic models such as Kuaishou Keling, MiniMax Hailuo AI, and ByteDance Seedance, Sora's leading advantage was quickly caught up and even surpassed, and user popularity declined. The business aspect has also changed. In December 2025, Disney invested $1 billion to license its IP for Sora video generation and planned to distribute related content on Disney+. However, according to foreign media reports, this cooperation has been cancelled along with the shutdown of Sora. Disney stated that it will continue to explore AI cooperation, but only on the premise of respecting copyright and creators' rights. A deeper change lies in the overall strategic shift of OpenAI. Several foreign media outlets previously disclosed that OpenAI is concentrating its resources on ChatGPT, attempting to create a "super app" that integrates chat, code, and creation capabilities, with video generation once considered for inclusion. However, in the trade-offs between resources and priorities, video generation has gradually been squeezed out of the core components. The Wall Street Journal also mentioned that OpenAI executive Fidji Simo, in an internal meeting, referred to projects like Sora as "side quests," emphasizing that resources needed to be returned to core products, such as the Agent system. This focus is also believed to be related to OpenAI's business streamlining in preparation for a potential IPO. Previously, internally, there had been questions about the large amount of computing resources consumed by Sora. OpenAI co-founder and CEO Sam Altman also revealed that the Sora team will shift its focus to long-term areas, such as robotics. Looking further ahead, OpenAI is also pushing forward with organizational restructuring and has pledged $1 billion through its foundation, covering areas such as life sciences, employment, and AI safety. Sora's exit seems more like a proactive choice in this shift in focus. Conclusion: From "Nuclear Bomb Release" to Being Removed from the Main Context Sora once defined the upper limit of AI video imagination with a "nuclear bomb release," but what truly influenced its future was perhaps computing power, product path, and company priorities. As resources gradually shifted towards agents, programming, and enterprise scenarios, Sora's position also changed. Under this round of internal adjustments, the product was ultimately shut down. That globally stunning "Sora moment" has thus come to an end.
